text: During the robotic radical cystoprostatectomy, the surgeon releases the endopelvic fascia on the left side over the prostate. Veins over the prostate are grasped to access the medial aspect of the lateral pelvic fascia or prostatic fascia. Retraction with the fourth robotic arm exposes the prostate, while some bleeding from the Santorini plexus veins is visible. The surgeon identifies the lateral pelvic fascia or prostatic fascia and uses counter traction with the left hand to find the optimal nerve-sparing plane, extending it apically and posteriorly around the prostate.

The surgeon releases the endopelvic fascia on the right side, medial to the veins over the prostate, using the fourth arm for retraction and the left hand for dissection and counter traction. The prostatic fascia is exposed for easier dissection with scissors. The nerves are dropped laterally, and dissection continues posteriorly along the back of the prostate to connect the right and left nerve sparing planes. A red vessel loop is used to demonstrate the complete connection of the nerve sparing plane posteriorly.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to grasp the bladder superiorly, initiating posterior dissection to target the vas deferens. Upon identifying the vas deferens, the seminal vesicle is located and freed using spot cautery. The freed seminal vesicles are retracted superiorly, and Denonvilliers' fascia is incised. A plane is created beneath the prostate and extended apically until encountering the red vessel loop, then dissected laterally to form a thin prostatic pedicle.

The surgeon ligates the lateral bladder pedicles, dissecting the blood vessels from the internal iliac artery lateral to the ureter. The bladder is retracted superiorly, and a vessel sealer is used to take down the bladder pedicles laterally. The prostate pedicle is reached, and the red vessel loop is grasped to thin out the pedicle, allowing for the application of a weck clip. Scissors are used to sharply transect the pedicle, completing the ligation with one to two weck clips. The vessel sealer is then used to ligate the posterior bladder pedicles, with retraction provided by the fourth arm, staying underneath the ureter to prevent positive margins.

The surgeon applies one to two weck clips to the dissected prostate pedicle. The bladder is dropped, and the urethra is transected, revealing the prostate, which is smooth and free of nerve tissue. Minor bleeding at the apex bed is controlled with hemostatic agents.
